---
title: "Chapter 5 — Drill planning under uncertainty"
subtitle: "Where should the next hole go? POMDP at regional scale + Gaussian process at property scale"
---

[← back to portfolio map](index.qmd)

## What this chapter is about

The first four chapters build prospectivity maps. A prospectivity
map is a per-cell probability surface — it doesn't tell you where
to drill *next* given what you've already drilled. Real exploration
campaigns are sequential: each hole costs money, returns information,
and that information should update the next-hole decision.

This chapter is the bridge from probability surface to hole-location
decision, at two scales:

- **Regional scale.** Given a per-cell prospectivity prior over
  thousands of square kilometers, where should we sequentially drill
  to maximize expected resource value while staying within a fixed
  budget? POMDP framing on the BCGT regional prior, solved with
  Monte Carlo Tree Search (POMCP).
- **Property scale.** Given a sparse grid of historical drill data,
  where should the next hole go to maximize information gain or
  expected grade? Gaussian-process regression on the Bear Cub
  placer property, plus two acquisition functions (max-variance for
  pure information gain, expected-improvement against a target
  grade).

Two different mathematical machineries, same intellectual move from
probability surface to hole-location decision.

## BCGT POMDP decision planning

The methodological reference is *Intelligent Prospector* v1.0 (Mern
et al., *Geosci. Model Dev.* 16:289, 2023). The setup:

- **State** = per-cell deposit indicator across a 30×30 working
  subarea (~15 km × 15 km centered on the BCGT prior's centroid of
  known occurrences). Cells are i.i.d. Bernoulli draws from the
  prior.
- **Observation** = noiseless reveal of the cell's true state when
  drilled. (v1.0 simplification; v2.0 adds measurement noise.)
- **Action** = drill cell (x, y), or stop and bank reward.
- **Reward** = $V per true positive drilled minus $C per drill.
- **Policy** = POMCP (Monte Carlo Tree Search over the belief MDP).

Three policies side-by-side on the same prior: random (uniform over
unrevealed cells), greedy (drill the highest-prior cell), and POMCP.
The POMCP planner gains roughly 10-15% in expected reward over
greedy at modest planner-depth budgets and substantially more at
deeper budgets. The lift is modest because the BCGT prior is
high-information already (much of the variance is captured by the
RF posterior), and because the v1.0 i.i.d.-Bernoulli + noiseless-
sensor assumptions limit how much MCTS rollouts can improve over
a one-step-lookahead greedy. v2.0 with correlated draws and noisy
sensors would widen the gap.

## Bear Cub Gaussian-process drill-site recommender

A different question on a different scale: the family's Bear Cub
placer property has 24 historical drill holes over a 597,000 ft²
patent (see [Chapter 1](bear_cub.qmd)). Given that sparse grid,
where should hole #25 go?

The model is a Gaussian-process regressor with an anisotropic RBF
kernel on (x, y) → predicted pay-zone grade. Two acquisition
functions:

- **Maximum predictive variance** picks the location maximizing the
  GP's posterior variance — drill where the model is most uncertain,
  because that's where a new measurement teaches the model the
  most.
- **Expected improvement** picks the location maximizing
  E[max(grade(x) − target, 0)] under the GP posterior with target =
  0.05 oz/yd³ — drill where the model thinks there might be a hit
  *and* it's not sure.

Both criteria exclude points within 80 ft of an existing hole.

![Recommended next-drill locations on the GP-predicted grade surface. Cyan triangles maximize predictive variance (info gain). Magenta stars maximize expected improvement against a 0.05 oz/yd³ target.](data/derived/bear_cub_resource/fig_gp_drill_recommender.png)

The two criteria recommend different locations: max-variance picks
the gaps in the existing drill grid (largest empty spaces between
holes), while expected-improvement picks gaps that are also near
already-known high-grade holes. For an exploration program with a
budget for one more hole, the EI criterion is what most planners
would use; for a research campaign focused on understanding the
deposit's overall shape, max-variance is more informative.

**Honest limit.** The spatial-only GP doesn't predict pay-zone
grade accurately on n=24 holes (leave-one-out R² is negative — the
model performs worse than just predicting the dataset mean). The
geological reason is that pay-zone grade has a sharp gradient at
the third beach line crossing the property; 24 spatial samples
can't constrain a gradient that steep. The recommender output is
**still meaningful even with a weak predictive model**: it uses the
GP's *uncertainty structure* (which is a faithful map of where
training data is sparse) rather than the GP's point predictions.
Max-variance picks remain the gaps in the grid; EI picks remain
gaps near known high-grade holes. The next hole would help the
GP fit the gradient.

## What these two pieces together demonstrate

The prospectivity work in chapters 2-4 produces probability
surfaces. A practical exploration team has a budget that converts
probability into hole-location decisions. The mathematical step
from probability to decision is the same intellectual move at both
scales, even though the machinery is very different (POMDP + MCTS
versus GP + acquisition function). Both pieces are at v1.0 fidelity
— the simplifications are documented in the underlying notebooks —
but the framing scales to real exploration campaigns with the
v2.0 extensions in the published literature.

## Deeper notebooks

- [Drill-planning chapter overview](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/drill_planning/index.html) — frames both pieces together.
- [BCGT POMDP decision planning](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/bcgt/decision_planning.html) — full notebook with POMCP implementation, random/greedy baselines, and the v1.0-vs-v2.0 limits discussion.
- [Bear Cub GP drill-site recommender](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/drill_planning/bear_cub_gp_recommender.html) — full notebook with both acquisition functions and the honest-limits discussion.

[← back to portfolio map](index.qmd)
